coredata.dc:title: A dual-channel fluorescent sensing system based on carbon dots and photoswitchable Pdots for sensitive detection of acetamiprid and carbendazim in food samples
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Limin
Hu, Xuelian
Pang, Wanyue
Cao, Ruoyu
Wang, Wenxiu
Liu, Xiuying
Tang, Yiwei
coredata.dc:description: The development of precise and efficient analysis technologies for acetamiprid (ACE) and carbendazim (CBZ) is of paramount importance for safeguarding the environment, ensuring food safety and protecting human health. In this study, we present a novel dual-channel fluorescent sensing system for the detection ACE and CBZ. This system integrates photoswitchable polymer dots (Pdots) and carbon dots (CDs) with aptamers and Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Specifically, when irradiated with 465 nm excitation light, only the Pdots probe emits a fluorescent signal. Conversely, when irradiated with 354 nm excitation light, the Pdots probe's fluorescence is quenched, and only the CDs probe emits a signal. This innovative design eliminates interference between the fluorescent probes, thereby enhancing the accuracy and sensitivity of the detection method. Based on competitive binding between the target analytes and the complementary strands (cDNA) immobilized on magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles with the fluorescent probes, the sensing system exhibited linear detection ranges of 1–100 ng/mL, with LODs of 0.33 ng/mL for ACE and 0.40 ng/mL for CBZ. We successfully applied this method to the analysis of ACE and CBZ in tap water, apple, and cucumber samples, achieving recoveries of 96.09 %–108.17 % and 94.59 %–109.09 %, respectively. This approach offers a highly promising tool for environmental monitoring and food safety analysis, significantly enhancing the capacity to monitor and manage potential health risks associated with these compounds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer dots
Carbon dots
Acetamiprid
Carbendazim
Magnetic separation
Fluorescence sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Aptamer or cDNA conjugation to nanoparticles
Determination of ACE and CBZ in real samples
Synthesis and characterization of photoswitching Pdots
Preparation and characterization of cDNA-modified Fe3O4
Preparation and characterization of aptamer-modified nanopaticles
Optimization of the fluorescence sensing system
Analytical performance of the fluorescence sensing method
Specificity and stability of the assay
Analysis of ACE and CBZ in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of BTE with open-ring and closed-ring (UV irradiated) and the fluorescence spectrum of MEH-PPV Pdots. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra and (c) fluorescence spectra of Pdots before and after photoswitching. (d) Schematic representation of the MEH-PPV/BTE integrated and their photoswitching reaction by UV/red light illumination. (e) TEM image and the particle size distribution histogram and (f) FT-IR spectra of Pdots (without PSMA), PSMA and Pdots. (g) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Pdots. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) TEM image and the particle size distribution histogram. (b) XRD and (c) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (d) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs. Inset: photographs of CDs solution under visible light (left) and UV light (right).

Fluorescence spectra of the sensing system in the presence of different concentrations of (a) ACE and (b) CBZ. The linear relationship between the enhanced fluorescent intensity and (c) ACE and (d) CBZ concentrations within the range of 1–100 ng/mL.
(a) Specificity analysis of the proposed fluorescence assay. (b) Stability of the sensing system.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of Pdots and CDs (a) and fluorescence sensing strategy for ACE and CBZ determination (b).
Determination of ACE and CBZ in tap water, cucumber and apple samples by the developed method and HPLC (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: P, P > 0.05 indicating no significant difference.

head.ce:title: A dual-channel fluorescent sensing system based on carbon dots and photoswitchable Pdots for sensitive detection of acetamiprid and carbendazim in food samples

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Limin Hou and Xuelian Hu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of precise and efficient analysis technologies for acetamiprid (ACE) and carbendazim (CBZ) is of paramount importance for safeguarding the environment, ensuring food safety and protecting human health. In this study, we present a novel dual-channel fluorescent sensing system for the detection ACE and CBZ. This system integrates photoswitchable polymer dots (Pdots) and carbon dots (CDs) with aptamers and Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Specifically, when irradiated with 465 nm excitation light, only the Pdots probe emits a fluorescent signal. Conversely, when irradiated with 354 nm excitation light, the Pdots probe's fluorescence is quenched, and only the CDs probe emits a signal. This innovative design eliminates interference between the fluorescent probes, thereby enhancing the accuracy and sensitivity of the detection method. Based on competitive binding between the target analytes and the complementary strands (cDNA) immobilized on magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles with the fluorescent probes, the sensing system exhibited linear detection ranges of 1–100 ng/mL, with LODs of 0.33 ng/mL for ACE and 0.40 ng/mL for CBZ. We successfully applied this method to the analysis of ACE and CBZ in tap water, apple, and cucumber samples, achieving recoveries of 96.09 %–108.17 % and 94.59 %–109.09 %, respectively. This approach offers a highly promising tool for environmental monitoring and food safety analysis, significantly enhancing the capacity to monitor and manage potential health risks associated with these compounds.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The escalating dependence on pesticides in agriculture has sparked increasing apprehension regarding their residues in food products, posing significant risks to human health and the environment [1]. Among the myriad pesticides currently in use, acetamiprid (ACE) and carbendazim (CBZ) have become two of the most employed agents. Acetamiprid, a neonicotinoid insecticide, is efficacious in controlling a broad spectrum of sucking pests, while carbendazim, a fungicide, offers robust protection against various fungal pathogens that can decimate crops [2,3]. However, the unregulated and excessive application of these pesticides has led to their inadvertent presence in the food chain, thereby posing a potential threat to human health and the environment. Even at low concentrations, these chemicals may have deleterious effects on human physiology, including endocrine disorder, genotoxic and (or) cytotoxic activities [4,5]. Consequently, the development of sensitive and reliable methods for detecting these residues in foodstuffs is of paramount importance.

Many existing detection methods for pesticide residues, such as liquid chromatography [6,7], surface Raman spectroscopy [8,9], and electrochemical methods [10,11], are highly accurate. However, they are often hampered by several limitations, including complex sample pretreatment procedures, expensive instrumentation, and time-consuming analysis, which restrict their application in on-site and rapid detection scenarios [12]. Immunological assays [13] and enzyme inhibition methods have been proposed to simplify the detection process, but these approaches often face challenges such as poor antibody stability, cross-reactivity, and relatively low sensitivity [14]. Therefore, there is a growing demand for rapid, sensitive, and cost-effective methods for detecting multiple pesticide residues simultaneously.

Fluorescence probe-based sensing methods have garnered significant attention as a promising approach due to their inherent advantages, including high sensitivity and selectivity, simplicity in operation, and rapid response [15,16]. In recent years, the integration of nanomaterials into fluorescence detection systems has revolutionized the field. Photoswitchable polymer dots (Pdots) have recently emerged as a promising class of fluorescent probes, attracting considerable attention [17,18]. These nanoscale materials possess unique photophysical characteristics, enabling them to reversibly switch their fluorescence emission in response to external light stimuli. This feature offers new opportunities for advanced sensing modalities [18]. Currently, photoswitchable Pdots are frequently employed in the domains of bio-imaging, cell sorting, and photodynamic therapy [19–21]. However, their application in food analysis, particularly for the detection of pesticide residues such as ACE and CBZ, remains largely underexplored. Carbon dots (CDs), a class of carbon-based nanomaterials, have garnered significant attention due to their exceptional fluorescence, low toxicity, good water solubility, and high chemical stability [22–24]. These properties have rendered them highly versatile for applications in sensing, bioimaging, catalysis, and drug delivery [25–28].

Aptamers, which are short oligonucleotide sequences that can bind to specific targets with high affinity and specificity, have increasingly been utilized in the detection of pesticides residues [29–31]. The combination of aptamers with fluorescence nanomaterials, such as CDs and peptide-based assemblies, has led to the creation of biosensors with enhanced sensitivity and selectivity [32,33]. Additionally, the incorporation of magnetic nanoparticles, such as Fe3O4, into sensing systems has been shown to enhance the separation and enrichment of target analytes, thereby improving detection limits and analysis speed [34–36]. The integration of these components offers a multimodal detection platform that leverages the strengths of each component for enhanced performance.

In light of the aforementioned studies, we developed a dual-channel fluorescence sensing system that integrates photoswitchable Pdots, CDs, aptamers, and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles for determination of ACE and CBZ (Scheme 1 b). This system represents a significant strategy in the field of pesticide detection, offering enhanced accuracy and sensitivity through its unique design and functional components. The prepared photoswitchable Pdots exhibit unique photophysical properties, with fluorescence quenching under UV light and reactivation under red light. This innovative feature effectively eliminates signal interference during detection, a common challenge in traditional fluorescence-based sensing systems. This property, combined with the strong fluorescence emission of CDs under UV light, allows for the development of a dual-channel fluorescence sensing system that significantly improves the accuracy and sensitivity of pesticide detection. Moreover, the aptamers ensure specific binding to the target pesticides, which is crucial for reliable detection in complex samples. The incorporation of Fe3O4 magnetic microspheres facilitates the separation and enrichment of the target analytes. This not only improves the detection limits but also enables rapid analysis, making the system highly practical for real-world applications.

Poly[2-methoxy-5-(2-ethylhexyloxy)-1,4-phenylenevinylene] (MEH-PPV, molecular weight: 26,000) was sourced from Xi'an Yuri Solar Co., Ltd. (Xi'an, China). The photochromic quencher, 1,2-bis(2,4-dimethyl-5-phenyl-3-thienyl)-3,3,4,4,5,5-hexafluoro-1-cyclopentene (BTE), was procured from Shanghai Mindray Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Poly(styrene-co-maleic anhydride) (PSMA, cumene-terminated, average Mn 1900 by GPC) was acquired from Rhawn Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), tetrahydrofuran (THF), and anhydrous sodium acetate were supplied by Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), l-arginine, CBZ, and poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Thiamethoxam (TMX) and chlorothalonil (CTL) were obtained from Acmec Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Nitenpyram (NTP) was purchased from Heowns Biochem Technologies, LLC. (Tianjin, China). Thiabendazole (TBZ) and ACE were sourced from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Thiophanate-methyl (TM) was acquired from Psaitong Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Imidacloprid (IMI) was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China).

The sequences of the ACE aptamer and its cDNA [37] were 5′–NH2–(CH2)6-CTG ACA CCA TAT TAT GAA GA-3′ and 5′–NH2–(CH2)6-TCT TCA TAA TAT GGT GTC AG-3′, respectively. Similarly, the sequences of the CBZ aptamer and its cDNA [38] were 5′–NH2–(CH2)6-GGG CAC ACA ACA ACC GAT GGT CCA GCC ACC CGA ATG ACC AGC CCA CCC GCC ACC CCG CG-3′ and 5′–NH2–(CH2)6- CGC GGG GTG GCG GGT GGG CTG GTC ATT CGG GTG GCT CCA CCA TCG GTT GTT GTG TGC CC-3′, respectively. Both sets of sequences were provided by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Fluorescence spectra were recorded using an F-320 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Guangdong Sci. &Tech., China) over the range of 200–900 nm. Morphological characterization was conducted using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with a FEI Tecnai F20 instrument (USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer (Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) data were acquired with a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 Fourier transform spectrometer (USA). Zeta potential measurements were carried out using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS ZEN3600 analyzer (UK).

The preparation of Pdots was conducted using the nanoprecipitation method [39]. Specifically, solutions of MEH-PPV (100 μL), BTE (300 μL), and PSMA (20 μL) in tetrahydrofuran (THF) at a concentration of 1 mg/mL were combined in a centrifuge tube. The mixture was uniformly dispersed by ultrasonication and then rapidly injected into 10 mL of ultrapure water. After 5 min of sonication, the THF solvent was removed through nitrogen stripping at 90 °C for 20 min. Then, the mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to eliminate aggregated particles and precipitates, and the purified Pdots solution was collected for further use.

The CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal synthesis method [40]. Initially, 0.2 g of l-arginine was dissolved in 40 mL of ultrapure water, and the mixture was sonicated for 20 min. The solution was then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 6 h, followed by cooling to room temperature. The resulting solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove any precipitate, yielding the CDs.

Carboxylated magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized using a one-pot method. Initially, 1.06 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 300 mg of PAA were introduced into 30 mL of ethylene glycol under vigorous stirring. Subsequently, 3.69 g of anhydrous sodium acetate was added and dissolved with continuous stirring until a uniform, sticky, orange-yellow solution was achieved. This solution was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 12 h. The resulting black product was isolated using an external magnetic field and washed three times with ethanol and ultrapure water. Finally, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles were obtained by drying in an oven at 50 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aptamer or cDNA conjugation to nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Nucleic acid-labeled nanoparticles were synthesized employing the classical carbodiimide method [41]. Typically, the fluorescence nanoparticles (2 mL of Pdots or 1 mL of CDs) were activated with a solution of EDC (50 μL, 10 mg/mL) and NHS (50 μL, 5 mg/mL) for 30 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the ACE aptamer (2.5 μL, 100 μmol/L) and CBZ aptamer (1.5 μL, 100 μmol/L) were incubated with the activated Pdots and CDs, respectively. After 4 h, the obtained Pdots@AptACE and CDs@AptCBZ solution was stored at 4 °C for further experiments.

The Fe3O4 nanoparticles (2 mg) was dispersed in 2 mL PBS buffer (0.01 mol/L, pH 7.4) under ultrasonic condition. Then, EDC (250 μL, 10 mg/mL) and NHS (250 μL, 5 mg/mL) solutions were added to the suspension. Subsequently, the cDNA (complementary to the ACE aptamer or CBZ aptamer) solution (3 μL, 100 μmol/L) was introduced, and the mixture was incubated for 4 h at room temperature. The resulting cDNA-linked Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fe3O4@cDNAACE and Fe3O4@cDNACBZ) were washed 3 times with PBS. The products were then redispersed in PBS and stored at 4 °C for future use.

A series of mixed standard solutions containing various concentrations of ACE and CBZ or sample extracts (200 μL) were prepared and subsequently mixed with 100 μL of Pdots@AptACE, 100 μL of CDs@AptCBZ, 10 μL of Fe3O4@cDNAACE, and 5 μL of Fe3O4@cDNACBZ. Following a 50 min incubation period, the magnetically responsive precipitate was isolated using an applied magnetic field and washed three times with PBS solution to remove any non-specifically bound species. The washed precipitate was then redispersed in 200 μL of PBS, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded in two distinct wavelength ranges: 320–540 nm and 560–750 nm. These measurements were performed upon excitation at 295 nm for the target ACE (Pdots@AptACE/Fe3O4@cDNAACE), and 465 nm for the target CBZ (CDs@AptCBZ/Fe3O4@cDNACBZ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ACE and CBZ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the dual-channel fluorescent sensing system, three samples (apple, cucumber and water) were selected and then spiked with ACE and CBZ at seven levels (0, 2, 4, 8, 20, 40 and 80 ng/mL or ng/mg). The samples were rinsed with ultrapure water and air-dried at room temperature for 24 h. The apple and cucumber samples were homogenized, and 2 g of each was mixed with 2 mL of acetonitrile. This mixture was vortexed for 5 min, and the supernatant was separated by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The extraction process was repeated twice. The combined supernatants were then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and the solvent was removed using a thermostat water bath. The residue was subsequently reconstituted in 2 mL of methanol for fluorescence detection. Tap water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, dried, and resolubilized in methanol for further analysis.

For HPLC analysis, the conditions for ACE were: mobile phase composed of methanol and 0.1 % formic acid aqueous solution, flow rate of 0.3 mL/min, column temperature of 40 °C, detection wavelength of 254 nm, and injection volume of 10 μL. For CBZ, the conditions were: mobile phase of methanol and water, flow rate of 1 mL/min, column temperature of 40 °C, detection wavelength of 286 nm, and injection volume of 10 μL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of photoswitching Pdots
ce:section.ce:para: Pdots were synthesized using BTE, MEH-PPV, and PSMA (Scheme 1a). Before UV irradiation, BTE displays a low-energy band at 574 nm, indicative of π→π∗ transitions of the open-ring isomers of dithienylethene (Fig. 1 a). Upon UV irradiation, the closed-ring isomer of BTE forms, exhibiting strong absorption bands at 574 nm, which partially overlap with the fluorescence emission spectrum of MEH-PPV Pdots (Fig. 1a). This overlap suggests that fluorescence resonance energy transfer can occur between the closed-ring BTE and MEH-PPV Pdots. Fig. 1b presents that the absorption peaks of the Pdots at 274 nm and 520 nm are attributed to BTE and MEH-PPV, respectively. Upon UV light irradiation, the absorbance peak at 274 nm decreases, indicating the transformation of BTE from its open-ring form to the closed-ring form. The absorption spectrum is restored upon red light irradiation, signifying the regeneration of the open-ring BTE [39]. As shown in Fig. 1c, the fluorescence intensity of the Pdots significantly decreases after UV irradiation and largely recovers upon red light irradiation, exhibiting satisfactory photoswitching properties (Fig. 1d).

Fig. 1e displays the TEM image of the Pdots, revealing that the synthesized Pdots are spherical in shape with an average diameter of 7.28 ± 2.89 nm. The surface functional groups of the Pdots were characterized using FT-IR (Fig. 1f). The FT-IR spectra of PSMA exhibit a CO stretching vibration peak in the range of 1778–1857 cm−1. Compared to Pdots without PSMA, a new characteristic absorption peak appeared at 1749 cm−1 in the spectra of the Pdots, which is attributed to the carboxyl groups formed from the hydrolysis of maleic anhydrides in the PSMA polymer during the nanoprecipitation process [42]. The schematic of the PSMA hydrolysis reaction is presented in Fig. S1a. These results confirm the presence of carboxyl groups on the surface of the Pdots, which can be utilized for bioconjugation.

The optical properties of the Pdots were characterized. Fig. S1b shows that the fluorescence intensity varies with the excitation wavelength ranging from 450 nm to 520 nm, peaking at an excitation wavelength of 465 nm. The maximum emission wavelength of the Pdots (the orange line in Fig. 1g) is located at 600 nm, with the excitation wavelength at 465 nm.

Optimizing reaction condition is crucial for achieving the desired fluorescence properties in the synthesis of CDs. The results demonstrate that a higher reaction temperature (180 °C) facilitates the formation of CDs with significantly enhanced fluorescence intensity (Fig. S2a). This enhancement is likely attributed to the increased rate of carbonization and the formation of more conjugated structures within the CDs at elevated temperatures. However, excessively high temperatures and prolonged reaction times can lead to the aggregation of small particles resulting from over-carbonization, thereby diminishing the fluorescence intensity of CDs [43]. As indicated in Fig. S2b, a reaction time of 6 h is sufficient for complete carbonization and the formation of a stable structure. Consequently, a reaction temperature of 180 °C and a reaction time of 6 h were selected for subsequent experiments.

The morphology and particle size distribution of the CDs were characterized. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, TEM images reveal that the CDs are spherical with an average diameter of 2.84 ± 0.75 nm. The XRD pattern exhibits a single broad peak at 2θ = 28.18°, which aligns with the (002) lattice spacing characteristic of carbon-based materials exhibiting substantial sp3 disorder (Fig. 2b) [40].

The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2c) provides insights into the surface functional groups of the CDs. The absorption peak at 3442 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H bonds, while the peak at 2870 cm−1 corresponds to the C–H stretching vibration. Peaks at 1649 cm−1, 1367 cm−1, and 1103 cm−1 were indicative of the stretching vibrations of the CO group in carboxylic acids, the C–N bond, and the C–OH bond, respectively [44]. These results confirm the presence of amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CDs.

Fig. 2d presents the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the prepared CDs. A prominent absorption peak at 292 nm is observed, which can be ascribed to the π-π∗ transition of CC and the n-π∗ transition of CO. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs varies with the excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 nm to 300 nm (Fig. S2c), peaking at 295 nm. Additionally, the CDs solution appears brown under visible light and emits blue light under UV illumination (Fig. 2d).

Transmission electron microscopy was utilized to characterize the morphology and size of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles. As observed in Fig. S3a, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles exhibit a spherical structure and are well dispersed, with an average diameter of 201.96 ± 47.32 nm. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S3b) shows an absorption peak at 584 cm−1, which is associated with the stretching vibration of the Fe–O functional group, confirming the successful synthesis of Fe3O4 materials [41]. The broad peak at 3402 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the O–H bond, while peaks at 1735 cm−1, 1633 cm−1, 1548 cm−1, and 1406 cm−1 are indicative of the stretching vibrations of the CO and COO− groups, respectively [45]. These results suggest the successful synthesis of carboxylated Fe3O4 nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of cDNA-modified Fe3O4
ce:section.ce:para: To maximize the coupling of Fe3O4 with cDNAACE or cDNACBZ, the amount of cDNA added was optimized. As illustrated in Fig. S4a and S4b, the absorption peaks at 260 nm progressively intensified with increasing cDNA addition and began to stabilize once the addition reached 3 μL. Thus, 3 μL of cDNA was chosen for preparation of Fe3O4@cDNAACE or Fe3O4@cDNACBZ. The zeta potential increased from −18.15 mV to −13.67 mV and −9.08 mV after coupling with cDNAACE and cDNACBZ, respectively (Fig. S4c). This increase suggests that the cDNA molecules have been successfully adsorbed onto the surface of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles [46].

The magnetic properties of Fe3O4@cDNA were investigated using a vibrating sample magnetometer. As depicted in Fig. S4d, the magnetic hysteresis loop exhibits a symmetrical 'S' shape, with residual magnetizations and coercive forces close to zero, indicating that Fe3O4@cDNA possesses superparamagnetism. This is consistent with the characteristic behavior of superparamagnetic nanoparticles, allowing them to respond rapidly to an external magnetic field without retaining magnetization once the field is removed [47]. Such properties make Fe3O4@cDNA highly suitable for applications in point -of -care testing scenarios.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of aptamer-modified nanopaticles
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent CDs (1 mL) and Pdots (2 mL), coupled with varying amounts of the aptamers, were mixed with Fe3O4@cDNAACE and Fe3O4@cDNACBZ, respectively. Magnetic nanocomposites (Pdots@AptACE/Fe3O4@cDNAACE and CDs@AptCBZ/Fe3O4@cDNACBZ) were then collected using an external magnetic field, and their fluorescence intensities were measured. Higher fluorescence intensity correlated with a greater number of aptamers coupled to the fluorescent nanospheres. As depicted in Fig. S4e, the fluorescence intensity peaked with 2.5 μL of AptACE and 1.5 μL of AptCBZ for binding to Pdots and CDs, respectively. Consequently, these amount of AptACE and AptCBZ were utilized in this study.

Fig. S4f displays the zeta potential of Pdots and CDs before and after aptamer conjugation. The zeta potential of Pdots shifted from −43.49 mV to −28.86 mV, and that of CDs from −31.05 mV to −24.58 mV. The shifts confirm the successful synthesis of Pdots@AptACE and CDs@AptCBZ. The fluorescence spectra of the probes were recorded both before and after binding with the aptamers. As shown in Fig. S4g and S4h, the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent nanoparticles remained virtually unchanged upon conjugation with the aptamers. This result demonstrates that the modification with aptamers has a negligible impact on the optical properties of the prepared CDs and Pdots [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the fluorescence sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of ACE and CBZ on the fluorescence intensity of Pdots@AptACE and CDs@AptCBZ were evaluated. As depicted in Fig. S5a and S5b, the fluorescence intensity of Pdots@AptACE remained largely unchanged in the presence of ACE, and similarly, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@AptCBZ exhibited no significant variation in the presence of CBZ. Consequently, we devised an indirect detection strategy that relies on the competitive binding of the target analytes (ACE and CBZ) and magnetic Fe3O4@cDNA to the fluorescence probes (Pdots@AptACE and CDs@AptCBZ). The detection procedure is illustrated in Scheme 1b. Upon incubation of the standard solution or sample extract containing Fe3O4@cDNA with the fluorescent probe, the probe preferentially and specifically binds to ACE and CBZ. Subsequently, Fe3O4@cDNA binds to the remaining unoccupied fluorescent probe. After magnetic separation, the complexes (Pdots@AptACE/Fe3O4@cDNAACE and CDs@AptCBZ/Fe3O4@cDNACBZ) are redispersed in PBS solution, and their fluorescence intensity is measured. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of these complexes decreases with increasing concentrations of the target analytes (ACE and CBZ).

To achieve optimal analytical performance, the added amount of Fe3O4@cDNA and incubation time were optimized. As is shown in Fig. S6a, the fluorescence signal intensified with increasing amounts of Fe3O4@cDNA. The fluorescence intensity of the sensing system peaked when 10 μL of Fe3O4@cDNAACE and 5 μL of Fe3O4@cDNACBZ were added, respectively. Beyond these amounts, the fluorescence intensity decreased, likely due to the Fe3O4 nanoparticles covering the fluorescence of Pdots and CDs. Therefore, 10 μL of Fe3O4@cDNAACE and 5 μL of Fe3O4@cDNACBZ were selected for subsequent experiments.

As depicted in Fig. S6b, the fluorescence intensity of the sensing system gradually increased with extended incubation time between Fe3O4@cDNA and aptamer-modified fluorescence nanoparticles, stabilizing after 50 min. Thus, 50 min was confirmed as the optimal incubation time for this assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the fluorescence sensing method
ce:section.ce:para:
Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence spectra of the sensing system were recorded following the addition of various concentrations of the target analytes (1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 9, 10, 20, 30, 50, 60, 70, 90, and 100 ng/mL). As observed in Fig. 3 a and b, the fluorescence intensity for Pdots and CDs decreased progressively with increasing target analyte concentrations. Fig. 3c and d demonstrate a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity (
Δ F =
F 0
− F
, where F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of target analytes, respectively) and the analyte concentration (1–100 ng/mL). The linear regression equations of ACE are ΔF = 30.99c ACE +88.19 (R2 = 0.9990, 1–10 ng/mL) and ΔF = 2.74c ACE +370.91 (R2 = 0.9985, 10–100 ng/mL). For CBZ, the equations are ΔF = 26.13c CBZ +343.56 (R2 = 0.9934, 1–10 ng/mL) and ΔF = 3.82c CBZ +562.60 (R2 = 0.9989, 10–100 ng/mL). According to the 3σ/S rule, where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurements (n = 9), and S is the slope of the calibration line, the LOD of the assay is 0.33 ng/mL for ACE and 0.40 ng/mL for CBZ.

Compared to previously reported assays for the determination of ACE and CBZ, the proposed fluorescence sensing method demonstrated a lower limit of detection (Table S1a and S1b). Importantly, the incorporation of photoswitchable Pdots enabled the fluorescent sensing system to overcome the mutual interference of detection signals among different fluorescent probes. Furthermore, the introduction of magnetic materials minimized the impact of the matrix on the detection results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and stability of the assay
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the specificity of the assay for ACE and CBZ, the fluorescence response to common insecticides and fungicides (TMX, NTP, IMI, TM, TBZ, CTL, 100 ng/mL) was examined (the structure as shown in Fig. S7). As shown in Fig. 4 a, ACE and CBZ (100 ng/mL) induced significant fluorescence changes, whereas other pesticides resulted in negligible alterations. These findings indicate that the sensing system exhibits high specificity.

The stability of the fluorescent sensing system was also assessed. Solutions of Pdots@AptACE, CDs@AptCBZ, Fe3O4@cDNAACE, and Fe3O4@cDNACBZ were stored at 4 °C and periodically retrieved to construct the sensing system and evaluate stability. As depicted in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity remained stable for at least 30 days, demonstrating the robust stability of the sensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ACE and CBZ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the applicability of the proposed assay, three matrices-apple, cucumber, and water-were spiked with a mixed solution of ACE and CBZ at various concentrations (0, 2, 4, 8, 20, 40 and 80 ng/mL or ng/mg) and analyzed. As detailed in Table 1 , the average recoveries for ACE ranged from 96.09 % to 108.17 %, and for CBZ from 94.59 % to 109.09 %. The relative standard deviations (RSD) were below 8.78 % for ACE and 8.96 % for CBZ. No significant differences (P > 0.05) were observed between the recoveries determined by the developed sensing assay and those by HPLC. Thus, the assay demonstrated sufficient accuracy for practical applications.

To assess the stability of the dual-channel fluorescent sensing system for detecting ACE and CBZ, tap water samples spiked with these analytes were analyzed over a 30-day period. As presented in Table S2, the recoveries for ACE ranged from 95.6 % to 104.2 %, and for CBZ from 92.3 % to 107.0 %, with the RSDs less than 9.1 %. These results indicated that the fluorescent sensing assay maintains good stability over the tested duration.

This study developed a dual-channel fluorescent sensing system using Pdots and CDs coupled with aptamers and Fe3O4 nanoparticles for the detection of ACE and CBZ. The system demonstrated high sensitivity, specificity, and stability, making it suitable for practical applications across various matrices. The use of fluorescent nanoparticles and aptamers provided a robust and reliable detection method, with remarkable long-term stability. Future work will explore the integration of this system into portable devices for on-site monitoring. Additionally, by altering the type of aptamer, the system's application can be expanded to detect other chemical hazards in environmental and food produces.

Limin Hou: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xuelian Hu: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Wanyue Pang: Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Ruoyu Cao: Validation, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Wenxiu Wang: Validation, Resources, Data curation. Xiuying Liu: Validation, Software, Resources. Yiwei Tang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Central Government to Guide Local Science and Technology Development, China Fund of Hebei Province (No. 246Z5503G), the Scientific Research Foundation for the Introduced Talent of Hebei Agricultural University, China (No. YJ2023017 and No. YJ201911), and Earmarked Fund for Hebei Agriculture Research System (HBCT2024150203).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Song, M. Lu, P. Liang, T. Li, X. Huang, Y. Ma, L. Wan, T. Wang, Smartphone-assisted ratiometric FRET aptasensor based on quantum dots and gold nanoparticles for point-of-care testing of zearalenone in cereals, Food Control 165 (2024) 110666, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodcont.2024.110666.
